title: Renal Artery Stenosis
chief_complaint: My blood pressure has been impossible to control. I'm on 4 different medications and it's still high. I developed kidney problems suddenly when my doctor started me on lisinopril. I also have episodes of fluid in my lungs that come and go.

hidden_data.renal_doppler: Right renal artery: Peak systolic velocity 280 cm/s (normal <180). Renal-aortic ratio 3.8 (normal <3.5). Turbulent flow. 85% stenosis of proximal right renal artery. Left kidney: Normal.
hidden_data.ct_angiography: 85% ostial atherosclerotic stenosis of right renal artery. Left renal artery patent. Right kidney size 8.5cm (atrophic). Left kidney 12.0cm (compensatory hypertrophy).
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 2.3 (baseline 1.2 before starting lisinopril 2 months ago), BUN 38, K+ 4.8
hidden_data.renin: Renin level elevated (plasma renin activity 12 ng/mL/hr - normal <4)

correct_diagnosis: Hemodynamically Significant Atherosclerotic Renal Artery Stenosis (Right-Sided, 85%) with Renin-Dependent Hypertension and ACEi-Induced AKI
key_learning_points: RAS = narrowing of renal artery, most commonly from atherosclerosis (90%) in older adults or fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) in young women
Clinical clues: Resistant HTN (≥3 meds), acute rise in Cr after starting ACEi/ARB (30% rise expected, but >50% indicates bilateral RAS or solitary kidney), flash pulmonary edema, abdominal bruit
Pathophysiology: Stenosis → decreased renal perfusion → renin-angiotensin activation → HTN. ACEi/ARB block efferent arteriole vasoconstriction → drop GFR in stenotic kidney
Diagnosis: Duplex ultrasound first (peak systolic velocity >200, RAR >3.5). CTA/MRA confirm. Captopril renography historical. Renal angiogram is gold standard

Treatment: Medical (ACEi/ARB if tolerated, statin, antiplatelet, BP control). Revascularization (stent) if: Resistant HTN, flash pulmonary edema, progressive CKD, bilateral RAS. CORAL trial: Stenting no better than medical therapy for most patients
